claims.claim: Section 4(5) of SOSMA 2012 requires Parliament to renew the preventive-detention provisions every 5 years.
claims.source: Official law text: Laws of Malaysia Act 747, Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; transcript checked at https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Security_Offences_(Special_Measures)_Act_2012 lines 247-260. Official 2024 amendment Act A1736, MOHA PDF, gazetted 1 Nov 2024: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/KESALAHAN%20KESELAMATAN/SECURITY%20OFFENCES%20SPECIAL%20MEASURES%20ACT%202012%20AMENDMENT%202024.pdf
claims.correction: The renewal/review clause is subsection 4(11), not 4(5). Subsection 4(5) is the provision allowing a police officer of or above Superintendent rank to extend detention for investigation for not more than 28 days. Subsection 4(11) says subsection 4(5) must be reviewed every five years and ceases to have effect unless both Houses pass a resolution extending it. The phrase 'preventive-detention provisions' should be narrowed to the 28-day investigative detention power in subsection 4(5).
claims.stale_check: Not a stale-change issue. The 2024 amendment deleted section 19 only and did not alter section 4(5) or 4(11), based on Act A1736.

claims.claim: Renewal motions produce shorter debates than the law's original passage.
claims.source: Parliament Hansard search located official 2012 Dewan Rakyat records around 17-18 Apr 2012 but I could not verify a complete duration comparison against each renewal debate from primary Hansard within reasonable effort. Example official Parliament Hansard URL attempted: https://www.parlimen.gov.my/files/hindex/pdf/DR-17042012.pdf
claims.correction: Either cite exact Hansard dates and debate durations for the 2012 bill and each renewal motion, or soften to 'available renewal reports indicate shorter debate than the original legislative package, but exact duration comparison needs Hansard verification.'

claims.claim: Civil society has called for substantive reform alongside renewal.
claims.source: Malaysian Bar press release, 'Repeal SOSMA in Its Entirety', 25 Mar 2022, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/press-statements/press-statements/press-release-repeal-sosma-in-its-entirety; Lawyers for Liberty press statement, 14 Dec 2022, https://www.lawyersforliberty.org/2022/12/14/lfl-ph-led-new-government-cannot-retain-defend-the-draconian-sosma-which-as-opposition-they-voted-against-in-parliament-earlier-this-year/
claims.correction: This is verified for repeal/review calls by the Malaysian Bar and Lawyers for Liberty. For 'alongside renewal' specifically, cite the Malaysian Bar's 25 Mar 2022 statement responding to Parliament's rejection of the subsection 4(5) extension.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue. Later statements in 2023-2025 continued to call for review/repeal.

claims.claim: SOSMA's 5-year renewal cycle produces a recurring legislative ritual: short debate, narrow attendance, no substantive review.
claims.source: Bernama, 'SOSMA Sub-section 4(5) extended for five years', 26 Jul 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117; official Act 747 subsection 4(11), AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf
claims.correction: The five-year cycle for subsection 4(5) is verified, and the July 2022 Dewan Rakyat vote had 111 in favour, 88 against and 21 absent, with 18 MPs debating. But 'short debate', 'narrow attendance' and 'no substantive review' need exact Hansard and committee-record support. 'No substantive review' is an analytical conclusion, not directly established by the cited vote report.
claims.stale_check: Possible stale/context issue: by 2025 Parliament's Special Select Committee had held sessions on proposed SOSMA amendments and heard SUARAM/SUHAKAM evidence, so a current 2026 claim of 'no substantive review' needs careful date-bounding.

claims.source: Official law text: Act 747 subsection 4(11), AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; transcript checked at https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Security_Offences_(Special_Measures)_Act_2012 lines 249-260
claims.correction: Subsection 4(11), not subsection 4(5), creates the five-year review/extension requirement for subsection 4(5).

claims.claim: The 2012 original received hours of debate; renewals have been substantially shorter.
claims.source: Official Parliament Hansard pages need exact extraction. Search located official Parliament Hansard around 2012, but complete timing comparison was not independently verified from primary records within reasonable effort.
claims.correction: Add exact Hansard citations with start/end times and member counts for the original 2012 bill and each renewal motion. Without that, remove or soften the claim.

claims.claim: The renewal mechanism is procedurally simple: a motion put to a vote, with debate time allocated by the Speaker and government business managers.
claims.source: Bernama, 26 Jul 2022, reports the motion was passed by Dewan Rakyat by bloc vote: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117; official Parliament Standing Orders page located but not fully fetched: https://www.parlimen.gov.my/peraturan-mesyuarat.html?lang=en&uweb=dr
claims.correction: The 'motion put to a vote' part is verified for 2022. The allocation of debate time by the Speaker/government business managers needs a precise Standing Orders citation. Also, subsection 4(11) requires a resolution by both Houses, not only Dewan Rakyat.

claims.claim: Suaram's data on SOSMA arrests (6,892 between 2018 and 2023, ~28% released without charge) is the strongest civil-society evidence on how the law actually operates.
claims.source: Malaysiakini report quoting SUARAM launch of Malaysia Human Rights Report 2025, 7 Apr 2026, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/772005; The Sun report, 8 Apr 2026, https://thesun-ipaper.cld.bz/20260408
claims.correction: The 6,892 figure found in current reporting is described as arrests since SOSMA's introduction in 2012, not between 2018 and 2023. Malaysiakini reports about 22% released without charge and quotes SUARAM as saying 20%-28%, not a clean '~28%' figure. The 'strongest civil-society evidence' characterization is evaluative and should be attributed, not stated as fact.
claims.stale_check: Important stale/context issue: the visible 6,892 figure appears in April 2026 reporting on SUARAM's 2025 report. If the card was drafted before Apr 2026, the figure could not have been available in that form; if derived on 17 May 2026, it should use the updated 'since 2012' framing.

claims.claim: Much of that data is not part of the formal renewal-debate record because it post-dates the original 2012 passage.
claims.source: Bernama, 26 Jul 2022 renewal vote report, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117; Malaysiakini, 7 Apr 2026 SUARAM 2025 report coverage, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/772005
claims.correction: Data after 2012 obviously could not have been part of the original 2012 passage, but the relevant question is whether it was part of later renewal or review records. If the data is from SUARAM's 2025 report, it also post-dates the 2022 renewal vote. The sentence should distinguish original passage, 2022 renewal, and later 2025-2026 review evidence.
claims.stale_check: Yes. Some review activity appears to have occurred after the 2022 renewal, including 2025 parliamentary committee discussion of Act 747, so current wording may be stale or incomplete.

claims.claim: Civil-society petitions have been submitted at multiple renewal moments, but renewal procedure does not require parliamentary acknowledgement of public submissions.
claims.source: Malaysian Bar press release, 25 Mar 2022, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/press-statements/press-statements/press-release-repeal-sosma-in-its-entirety; Lawyers for Liberty press statements, 14 Dec 2022 and 16 Dec 2022, https://www.lawyersforliberty.org/2022/12/14/lfl-ph-led-new-government-cannot-retain-defend-the-draconian-sosma-which-as-opposition-they-voted-against-in-parliament-earlier-this-year/ and https://www.lawyersforliberty.org/2022/12/16/lfl-anwar-ibrahim-being-a-former-victim-of-custodial-abuse-must-intervene-in-home-ministers-refusal-to-review-sosma/
claims.correction: I verified public statements/interventions by civil society, but not formal 'petitions submitted' at multiple renewal moments. The procedural claim about no required parliamentary acknowledgement needs a Standing Orders or parliamentary procedure citation.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue identified, but the claim needs exact procedural sourcing.

claims.claim: The Bar Council, Suaram, Lawyers for Liberty and others have all documented their interventions in successive renewal cycles.
claims.source: Malaysian Bar press releases 7 Nov 2019 and 25 Mar 2022, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/about-us/president-s-corner/pressstatements/press-release-repeal-sosma-ndash-recognise-right-to-a-fair-trial-and-the-rule-of-law and https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/press-statements/press-statements/press-release-repeal-sosma-in-its-entirety; Lawyers for Liberty press statement 14 Dec 2022, https://www.lawyersforliberty.org/2022/12/14/lfl-ph-led-new-government-cannot-retain-defend-the-draconian-sosma-which-as-opposition-they-voted-against-in-parliament-earlier-this-year/; SUARAM coverage, The Star, 16 Feb 2025, https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2025/02/16/suaram-abolish-sosmas-28-day-detention-bail-denial
claims.correction: Verified that these groups documented interventions on SOSMA across different years. Not verified that each intervention corresponded to 'successive renewal cycles.' Use 'successive years and review moments' unless exact renewal-cycle documents are cited.

claims.claim: The renewal mechanism itself is not designed to revisit whether the law should still exist.
claims.source: Act 747 subsection 4(11), official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; transcript checked at https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Security_Offences_(Special_Measures)_Act_2012 lines 249-260
claims.correction: This is a reasonable inference from subsection 4(11), which reviews only subsection 4(5), not SOSMA as a whole. State it as an inference from the statutory text rather than a standalone procedural fact.

claims.claim: Renewal procedure is not the same as substantive review.
claims.source: Act 747 subsection 4(11), official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; Bernama renewal vote report, 26 Jul 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117
claims.correction: This is an analytical conclusion supported by the limited scope of subsection 4(11), but it should be worded as analysis, not a directly sourced legal rule.

claims.claim: A law permitting detention without trial deserves substantive review at every renewal, anchored on Suaram's arrest and release-without-charge data.
claims.source: Act 747 subsection 4(5) and 4(11), official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; Malaysiakini SUARAM 2025 report coverage, 7 Apr 2026, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/772005
claims.correction: Normative 'deserves' claim is not fact-checkable. The factual foundation should say SOSMA permits up to 28 days' investigative detention without magistrate remand under subsection 4(5), and the SUARAM figures should be corrected to '6,892 since 2012' and 'about 22% or 20%-28% released without charge' depending on the cited formulation.
claims.stale_check: The SUARAM figure appears updated in 2026 coverage and should not be back-applied to earlier renewal debates without date context.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Section 4(5) of SOSMA 2012 requires Parliament to renew the preventive-detention provisions every 5 years.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The exact subsection is wrong. Section 4(5) creates the 28-day detention extension power; subsection 4(11) creates the five-year review/extension requirement.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Act 747, Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: SOSMA's 5-year renewal cycle produces a recurring legislative ritual: short debate, narrow attendance, no substantive review.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The claim compresses a legal renewal requirement, attendance numbers, debate duration, and quality of review into a categorical conclusion without primary Hansard support. Bernama verifies one 2022 vote count and 18 debating MPs, not the broader recurring pattern.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The 2012 original received hours of debate; renewals have been substantially shorter.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Exact debate-duration comparison was not verified from primary Hansard records. This needs start/end times for the original bill and each renewal motion.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Official Parliament Hansard records required; attempted URL https://www.parlimen.gov.my/files/hindex/pdf/DR-17042012.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Suaram's data on SOSMA arrests (6,892 between 2018 and 2023, ~28% released without charge) is the strongest civil-society evidence on how the law actually operates.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Current accessible reporting says 6,892 arrests since 2012, not 2018-2023, and reports about 22% or a range of 20%-28% released without charge. The 'strongest' label is unsupported opinion.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Much of that data is not part of the formal renewal-debate record because it post-dates the original 2012 passage.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This is trivially true for the 2012 original passage but elides the more relevant question of whether data was before or after later renewal and review moments. If the 6,892 figure comes from a 2025/2026 SUARAM report, it post-dates the 2022 renewal as well.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Civil-society petitions have been submitted at multiple renewal moments, but renewal procedure does not require parliamentary acknowledgement of public submissions.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Public statements were verified, but formal petitions and the procedural rule on acknowledgement were not verified from primary parliamentary procedure documents.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Malaysian Bar, 25 Mar 2022, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/press-statements/press-statements/press-release-repeal-sosma-in-its-entirety; official Standing Orders citation still required

omitted_facts: Subsection 4(11), not subsection 4(5), is the five-year review/extension clause; subsection 4(5) itself is the power to extend investigative detention up to 28 days. Source: Act 747 official AGC PDF URL https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf

The 2022 renewal sequence included an initial Dewan Rakyat defeat in March 2022 and a later extension vote in July 2022. Bernama reports the July motion passed with 111 MPs agreeing, 88 disagreeing and 21 absent, and that the March motion had 85 agreeing and 86 disagreeing. Source: Bernama, 26 Jul 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117

Bernama reported 18 MPs took part in the July 2022 debate. This complicates any claim of 'narrow debate' unless compared against exact Hansard durations and participant counts. Source: Bernama, 26 Jul 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117

The 2024 Security Offences (Special Measures) (Amendment) Act 2024, Act A1736, deleted section 19 and was published in the Gazette on 1 Nov 2024. This should be noted when discussing the current Act. Source: MOHA official PDF, https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/KESALAHAN%20KESELAMATAN/SECURITY%20OFFENCES%20SPECIAL%20MEASURES%20ACT%202012%20AMENDMENT%202024.pdf

The government reported engagement sessions for proposed SOSMA amendments in 2023. Source: Bernama, 'Twelve Engagement Sessions Held For Proposed Sosma Amendment - Ramkarpal', https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2212824

Current accessible reports of SUARAM's 2025 report state the 6,892 arrests figure as since SOSMA's introduction in 2012, with about 22% or 20%-28% released without charge, not 'between 2018 and 2023, ~28%'. Source: Malaysiakini, 7 Apr 2026, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/772005
source_assessment: The original analysis lists no sources, so source diversity is effectively zero. In verification, the strongest sources were the statutory text of Act 747 and Act A1736, Bernama reports on parliamentary votes, and civil-society primary statements from the Malaysian Bar and Lawyers for Liberty. The original cards omit government/security justifications, Dewan Negara records, exact Hansard timing, court jurisprudence, detainee/family testimony, SUHAKAM material, and the 2023-2025 reform-review context. Political spectrum coverage is therefore thin and rights-advocacy heavy.
overall_assessment: The analysis has a defensible broad theme that SOSMA's five-year extension process concerns the 28-day detention power and is narrower than a full review of the Act. However, it contains a serious legal citation error by attributing the renewal requirement to section 4(5) instead of subsection 4(11), uses an apparently wrong date range for the SUARAM arrest figure, and makes several claims about debate length, attendance and petitions without primary-source support. It should not be published without correcting the legal section, re-sourcing the statistics, and adding Hansard-based evidence for the debate-duration claims.
